title: Iron oxide wash on bisqueware
subtitle: Brushing a diluted iron oxide wash onto bisque-fired stoneware
excerpt: A diluted iron oxide wash is brushed across the surface of bisque-fired stoneware and then wiped back to leave colour in recesses while the raised surfaces remain lighter. The technique enhances texture and carving and fires to amber, brown, or near-black depending on concentration.
sourceNotes: Iron oxide wash on bisqueware technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Ceramic work that has been through a first low-temperature firing to harden it without glaze. Bisqueware is porous and ready to accept glaze or oxide washes.
A naturally occurring mineral pigment used in ceramics. Red iron oxide fires to amber, rust-brown, or near-black depending on concentration and kiln atmosphere.
A very diluted solution of oxide or colourant brushed over the ceramic surface. A wash settles into recesses and can be wiped back from raised areas.

content.text: is one of the simplest and most useful surface treatments in stoneware glazing. Applied to
before the glaze layer, it accents carving, impressed decoration, and thrown texture. The
settles into recesses and is wiped back from raised areas, creating tonal contrast that survives the glaze firing.

attrs.content: Wear an N95 mask throughout iron oxide work. Iron oxide is a fine mineral powder — inhaling ceramic dust causes long-term lung damage. Use a dedicated mixing bucket and work surface. Never sweep dry oxide dust; wipe up wet. Do not mix raw glaze materials at home without a P100 respirator and proper ventilation.

items.name: Red iron oxide (pre-mixed wash or powder)
Sponge or cloth for wiping back

content.text: Mix iron oxide with water in a dedicated container to a thin, milky consistency, roughly one part oxide to eight parts water for a medium tone. A thinner wash gives amber-brown tones; thicker gives darker browns. If using a pre-mixed oxide wash, dilute following the manufacturer's guidance. Stir before each use as oxide settles rapidly.
Place the bisqueware piece on the banding wheel. Put on the N95 mask.
Load the mop brush and brush the wash generously across the entire surface in broad strokes, spinning the banding wheel as you work. The bisqueware will absorb the wash quickly.
Allow the wash to absorb for 2-3 minutes until the surface appears dry.
Dampen a sponge and wipe gently across the raised areas to remove the surface wash, leaving oxide concentrated in recesses and texture. The raised areas will appear lighter than the recessed areas.

Allow the washed piece to dry completely (typically 30-60 minutes) before applying a glaze coat over the top. A transparent or semi-transparent glaze preserves the oxide contrast. An opaque glaze will cover the oxide effect. Apply glaze by dipping, brushing, or pouring as usual.
Apply the wash without wiping back to achieve a uniform overall amber tone beneath a clear glaze.
Use cobalt oxide in place of iron oxide for a blue-grey wash on pale stoneware.

items.symptom: Wash wipes back too easily, leaving almost no colour
items.cause: Wash too dilute or bisqueware surface very porous after a hot bisque firing
items.fix: Mix the oxide wash slightly thicker. Alternatively dampen the bisqueware surface lightly before applying the wash so the pores do not absorb the oxide too deeply

items.symptom: Wash does not wipe back cleanly; dark smears remain on raised areas
items.cause: Wash dried too long before wiping, or was applied too thickly
items.fix: Wipe back within 5 minutes of application, while the oxide layer is still slightly damp
